chunk_summary: Guidelines for managing newborn weight loss include structured feeding assessments, supplementation steps, and criteria for stopping supplementation to ensure effective milk transfer and weight gain.
learning_objectives: Understand the clinical signs and thresholds for intervention in newborn weight loss, including 10% birth weight loss and signs of dehydration.
Learn the stepwise approach to supplementation, prioritizing expressed breast milk, donor milk, and formula.
Identify criteria for stopping supplementation, including effective milk transfer, weight gain trends, and caregiver confidence.
Recognize when to escalate care for persistent weight loss or hydration issues.
key_facts: Weight loss approaching 10% of birth weight triggers urgent feeding review.
Supplementation hierarchy: expressed breast milk > donor human milk > formula.
Preferred feeding methods: cup/spoon or paladai to protect breastfeeding skills.
Reassess within 24–48 hours to monitor urine output, feeding calmness, and weight stabilization.
Stopping criteria: effective milk transfer, weight gain trend, and caregiver confidence.
Escalate care for maternal delayed lactogenesis, infant oral-motor issues, or metabolic disease.
reference_quote: Early weight loss requires structured assessment rather than reflex formula use. Trigger urgent feeding review when weight loss approaches 10% of birth weight, or when clinical dehydration signs, jaundice, or hypoglycemia suggest inadequate intake. (Source: Stanford Medicine)
document_key_topics: Newborn weight loss thresholds and clinical signs
Escalation of care for persistent feeding difficulties
